text: the following video is of laparoscopic selenectomy from government medical college srinagar post graduate department of surgery and critical care medicine in the short video that you are about to see We are demonstrating laparoscopic splenectomy.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the omentum in front of the spleen, identifying two distinct tissue layers. The harmonic scalpel provides both hemostatic and dissecting capabilities. An Enseal device is employed near the stomach to manage the short gastric vessels. The splenic artery is visible at the six o'clock position.

The surgeon uses the Enseal device to dissect the short gastric vessels away from the stomach, ensuring hemostasis. Attention is then turned to the hilum of the spleen, where the endoshears are used to dissect the vasculature. A vessel is identified, skeletonized, and ligated proximally with hemolock clips.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect between the vessels, revealing the splenic vein and branches of the splenic artery. The splenic artery is noted to have an early branching pattern, complicating the dissection. The surgeon carefully lifts the splenic artery and its branches, applying hemolock clips for ligation.
The surgeon uses a right angle dissector to isolate the main branch of the splenic artery, followed by clipping the artery. Harmonic shears are then employed to sever the artery from the hilum.

The surgeon applies pressure to the superior aspect of the spleen, facilitating blood flow back into circulation. Subsequently, the splenic vein is addressed to prevent vascular congestion within the spleen.

The surgeon uses endoshears to dissect the hilum towards the superior pole of the spleen, carefully separating the spleen from the hilum to avoid injury to surrounding structures. Adhesions on the posterior aspect of the spleen are dissected free, allowing the spleen to be lifted off the hilum.
The surgeon dissects the adhesions near the superior pole of the spleen, freeing the spleen from surrounding tissues.
